content.content:
55→| Layer | Technology | Rationale |
    56→|---|---|---|
    57→| **Content** | Markdown files in repo | Edited in VS Code, built at deploy time, version-controlled. No CMS dependency. (TECH-03 resolved) |
    58→| **Frontend** | Next.js (App Router, static export + client-side hydration) | React ecosystem, excellent static generation, server components for OG tags, Vercel-native |
    59→| **API** | Next.js API Routes (serverless functions on Vercel) | Co-located with frontend, zero cold-start on Vercel, TypeScript throughout |
    60→| **Database** | PostgreSQL (managed — Neon) | ACID transactions required for bid atomicity. Auction-engine tables only — no painting content. (TECH-04 resolved: Neon, no Supabase) |
    61→| **Bid status updates** | Client-side polling (30s interval) | Bids happen a few times per day. Polling eliminates WebSocket infrastructure entirely. (TECH-04 resolved) |
    62→| **Payments** | Stripe | PCI-compliant (HC-05), handles credit card + escrow-like holds, proven for marketplaces |
    63→| **Auth** | NextAuth.js (Auth.js) | Google/Apple OAuth + email/password, session management |
    64→| **Email** | Resend or Postmark | Transactional email with high deliverability |
    65→| **SMS** | Twilio | Outbid and closing warning notifications |
    66→| **CDN / Hosting** | Vercel | Global CDN, edge functions, zero-config deployment from Git |
    67→| **Image storage** | Vercel Blob + Next.js Image Optimization | 52 source images + 52 OG variants. On-demand resizing/format conversion. See §13.2. |
    68→| **Monitoring** | Vercel Analytics + Sentry | Error tracking, performance monitoring |
    69→
    70→### 2.2 Service Boundaries
    71→
    72→```
    73→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    74→│                         Vercel CDN                            │
    75→│  Static gallery pages (from markdown), images, JS bundles     │
    76→└──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
    77→                           │
    78→┌──────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────┐
    79→│                    Next.js Application                         │
    80→│                                                               │
    81→│  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌────────────┐ │
    82→│  │  Gallery Layer    │  │  Server Pages    │  │    API     │ │
    83→│  │  (SSG from .md)   │  │  (SSR for OG)   │  │   Routes   │ │
    84→│  │                   │  │                  │  │  (auction   │ │
    85→│  │  No database.     │  │                  │  │   engine)  │ │
    86→│  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘  └─────┬──────┘ │
    87→└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┘
    88→                                                     │
    89→                    ┌────────────────────────────────┼──────────┐
    90→                    │                                │          │
    91→          ┌─────────▼────────┐          ┌────────────▼──┐      │
    92→          │   PostgreSQL DB   │          │   Stripe API   │      │
    93→          │      (Neon)       │          │   (Payments)   │      │
    94→          │                   │          └───────────────┘      │
    95→          │  bidders, bids,   │                                 │
    96→          │  rewards, auction,│          ┌───────────────┐      │
    97→          │  wta_bids,        │          │  Notification  │      │
    98→          │  notifications    │          │  Queue (Email/ │      │
    99→          │                   │          │  SMS/Push)     │      │
   100→          └───────────────────┘          └───────────────┘      │
   101→                                                                │
   102→  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   103→  │  Client-side polling (30s) ← GET /api/bid-status
   104→  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   105→```
   106→
   107→### 2.3 Gallery / Auction Engine Separation
   108→
   109→| Concern | Gallery Layer | Auction Engine |
   110→|---|---|---|
   111→| **Data source** | Markdown files in `content/` | PostgreSQL (Neon) |
   112→| **When it runs** | Build time (static HTML) | Runtime (serverless functions) |
   113→| **What it serves** | Painting pages, series pages, homepage, about, how bidding works | Bid placement, bid status, dashboard, payments, auth, admin |
   114→| **Infrastructure** | None (static files on CDN) | Database + API routes + Stripe + auth |
   115→| **Post-auction** | Stands alone as a permanent archive | Database can be deleted |
   116→
   117→The gallery layer reads painting content from markdown at build time. The auction engine reads only what it needs for bid validation (painting slugs + dimensions → tier prices) from a thin reference table seeded from the same markdown source.
   118→
   119→### 2.4 Why Not a Full Backend Framework?
   120→
   121→A traditional server (Express, Django, Rails) would work but adds operational complexity disproportionate to the workload. This auction serves ~52 products over 6 months. The critical operations (placing a bid, processing payment, calculating WTA) happen infrequently. Serverless functions handle these spikes efficiently without maintaining idle infrastructure.
   122→
   123→---
   124→
   125→## 3. Database Schema
   126→
   127→The database serves the **auction engine only**. Painting content (titles, narratives, images, series) lives in markdown files and is rendered at build time — it does not belong in the database. The database stores what's transactional: bids, bidders, payments, rewards, auction state, and notifications.
   128→
   129→A thin `paintings` reference table provides the auction engine with the data it needs for bid validation and price computation. It is seeded from the markdown source files at deploy time and contains no content — only dimensions and computed prices.
   130→
   131→### 3.1 Table: `paintings` (reference — seeded from markdown)
   132→
   133→```sql
   134→CREATE TABLE paintings (

| Layer | Technology | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Content** | Markdown files in repo | Edited in VS Code, built at deploy time, version-controlled. No CMS dependency. (TECH-03 resolved) |
| **Frontend** | Next.js (App Router, static export + client-side hydration) | React ecosystem, excellent static generation, server components for OG tags, Vercel-native |
| **API** | Next.js API Routes (serverless functions on Vercel) | Co-located with frontend, zero cold-start on Vercel, TypeScript throughout |
| **Database** | PostgreSQL (managed — Neon) | ACID transactions required for bid atomicity. Auction-engine tables only — no painting content. (TECH-04 resolved: Neon, no Supabase) |
| **Bid status updates** | Client-side polling (30s interval) | Bids happen a few times per day. Polling eliminates WebSocket infrastructure entirely. (TECH-04 resolved) |
| **Payments** | Stripe | PCI-compliant (HC-05), handles credit card + escrow-like holds, proven for marketplaces |
| **Auth** | NextAuth.js (Auth.js) | Google/Apple OAuth + email/password, session management |
| **Email** | Resend or Postmark | Transactional email with high deliverability |

| **CDN / Hosting** | Vercel | Global CDN, edge functions, zero-config deployment from Git |
| **Image storage** | Vercel Blob + Next.js Image Optimization | 52 source images + 52 OG variants. On-demand resizing/format conversion. See §13.2. |
| **Monitoring** | Vercel Analytics + Sentry | Error tracking, performance monitoring |

### 2.2 Service Boundaries

          │                   │          │  SMS/Push)     │      │
          └───────────────────┘          └───────────────┘      │
                                                                │
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  │  Client-side polling (30s) ← GET /api/bid-status
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

### 2.3 Gallery / Auction Engine Separation

| Concern | Gallery Layer | Auction Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Data source** | Markdown files in `content/` | PostgreSQL (Neon) |
| **When it runs** | Build time (static HTML) | Runtime (serverless functions) |
| **What it serves** | Painting pages, series pages, homepage, about, how bidding works | Bid placement, bid status, dashboard, payments, auth, admin |
| **Infrastructure** | None (static files on CDN) | Database + API routes + Stripe + auth |
| **Post-auction** | Stands alone as a permanent archive | Database can be deleted |

The gallery layer reads painting content from markdown at build time. The auction engine reads only what it needs for bid validation (painting slugs + dimensions → tier prices) from a thin reference table seeded from the same markdown source.

### 2.4 Why Not a Full Backend Framework?

A traditional server (Express, Django, Rails) would work but adds operational complexity disproportionate to the workload. This auction serves ~52 products over 6 months. The critical operations (placing a bid, processing payment, calculating WTA) happen infrequently. Serverless functions handle these spikes efficiently without maintaining idle infrastructure.

---

## 3. Database Schema

The database serves the **auction engine only**. Painting content (titles, narratives, images, series) lives in markdown files and is rendered at build time — it does not belong in the database. The database stores what's transactional: bids, bidders, payments, rewards, auction state, and notifications.

A thin `paintings` reference table provides the auction engine with the data it needs for bid validation and price computation. It is seeded from the markdown source files at deploy time and contains no content — only dimensions and computed prices.

### 3.1 Table: `paintings` (reference — seeded from markdown)

file.content:
### 13.1 Painting Catalog (Markdown Content)

The painting catalog lives as markdown files in the codebase. Each painting is a single `.md` file with YAML frontmatter for structured data and body content for the narrative.

**Content structure:**

```
content/
  paintings/
    acceptance.md
    anger.md
    bargaining.md
    ... (52 files)
  series/
    grief.md
  pages/
    about.md
    how-bidding-works.md
    artist-statement.md
```

**Painting markdown format:**

```markdown
---
title: Acceptance
slug: acceptance
width_inches: 48
height_inches: 72
series: grief
series_order: 6
display_order: 1
size_category: oversized
shipping_estimate_low: 200
shipping_estimate_high: 500
image: acceptance.jpg
alt_text: "Acceptance, acrylic on canvas, 48\" × 72\""
video_url: https://...
---

[Narrative content — William writes this in VS Code as markdown prose]
```

**Build-time processing:**

1. Next.js reads all `content/paintings/*.md` at build time
2. Frontmatter is parsed into structured data for page generation (titles, dimensions, tier prices computed from dimensions, images, series grouping)
3. Narrative body is rendered as HTML
4. Static pages are generated: one per painting (`/paintings/[slug]`), gallery pages, series pages
5. A deploy-time script also seeds/upserts the thin `paintings` reference table in the database (slug + dimensions only — §3.1)

**Editing workflow:** William edits markdown files in VS Code. Push to git. Vercel auto-deploys. Live within minutes. No CMS, no admin panel, no database migration.

### 13.2 Image Infrastructure

file.content:
## 9. Deployment

### 9.1 Environments

| Environment | Purpose | URL |
|---|---|---|
| **Development** | Local development | `localhost:3000` |
| **Staging** | Pre-production testing | `staging.fhoa.org` |
| **Production** | Live auction | `fhoa.org` |

### 9.2 Deployment Pipeline

```
Git push → Vercel auto-deploy → Build (Next.js) → Deploy to edge
```

- **Preview deployments:** Every PR gets a unique preview URL
- **Production deployment:** Merges to `main` auto-deploy to production
- **Rollback:** Vercel instant rollback to any previous deployment

### 9.3 Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `DATABASE_URL` | PostgreSQL connection string | `postgresql://...` |
| `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` | Stripe API key | `sk_live_...` |
| `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET` | Webhook signature verification | `whsec_...` |
| `STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY` | Client-side Stripe.js | `pk_live_...` |
| `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` | Session encryption | Random 32+ char string |
| `NEXTAUTH_URL` | Canonical URL | `https://fhoa.org` |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` | Google OAuth | `...apps.googleusercontent.com` |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | Google OAuth | `GOCSPX-...` |
| `APPLE_CLIENT_ID` | Apple OAuth | `org.fhoa.signin` |
| `APPLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | Apple OAuth | JWT |
| `RESEND_API_KEY` | Email delivery | `re_...` |
| `TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID` | SMS delivery | `AC...` |
| `TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN` | SMS delivery | Token |
| `TWILIO_FROM_NUMBER` | SMS sender | `+1...` |
| `ADMIN_EMAIL` | Admin role check | William's email |

### 9.4 Domain Configuration

- `fhoa.org` → Vercel (A record or CNAME)
- SSL: Automatic via Vercel (Let's Encrypt)
- `www.fhoa.org` → redirect to `fhoa.org`

### 9.5 Auction Close Trigger

The auction close is the single most critical automated event in the system. It must execute reliably at `2026-12-31T23:59:59-05:00`.

content.text:
Here's the picture.

## Where the app lives in the repo

The spec puts the Next.js project inside `company/production/auction-platform/make/` — that's the "make" folder under the product. The content directory sits inside the app:

```
company/production/auction-platform/
  define/          ← specs (what we have now)
  learn/           ← research, prototypes
  make/            ← the actual codebase
    content/
      paintings/   ← 52 markdown files (acceptance.md, anger.md, ...)
      series/      ← series narratives (grief.md, ...)
      pages/       ← about.md, how-bidding-works.md, artist-statement.md
    src/
      app/         ← Next.js App Router pages
      components/  ← React components
      lib/         ← shared utilities, DB client, Stripe client
    scripts/       ← deploy-time seeding, OG image generation
    public/        ← static assets (favicon, etc.)
    package.json
    next.config.ts
    ...
```

The `content/` folder is where you work. Each painting is one markdown file with YAML frontmatter (title, dimensions, series, image reference) and a body you write as prose. Push to git, site updates.

## Deployment — yes, it's fully specced

The spec calls for **Vercel**, and the deployment model is straightforward:

- **Push to `main`** → Vercel auto-builds → static gallery pages generated from markdown → serverless API routes deployed → live at `fhoa.org`
- **Every PR** gets a preview URL for testing
- **Rollback** is instant (Vercel keeps every deployment)
- **Cost:** ~$20-30/month (Vercel Pro $20 + Neon free tier + Stripe per-transaction)
- **Post-auction:** drop to Vercel Hobby ($0), delete the database, the gallery stands alone as a static archive

Domain is `fhoa.org`. SSL automatic. No servers to manage.

## Build plan

The spec is the blueprint — I'd build it in roughly this order:

1. **Scaffold** — Next.js project in `make/`, TypeScript, Tailwind, basic config
2. **Content layer** — markdown parsing pipeline (frontmatter → pages), one sample painting file, gallery page rendering
3. **Static pages** — homepage, painting detail, series page, about, how bidding works (all reading from `content/`)
4. **Database + auth** — Neon connection, schema migration (6 tables), NextAuth setup
5. **Auction engine** — bid placement API, bid status polling, dashboard
6. **Payments** — Stripe integration, escrow-style holds
7. **Notifications** — email (Resend), SMS (Twilio) for outbid/win
8. **WTA + settlement** — Winner Takes All logic, auction close trigger, reward disbursement
9. **Admin** — lawyer-mediated bids, manual overrides
10. **Polish** — OG images, responsive, performance, Sentry

Steps 1-3 are purely static — you could see your paintings on a website before any database or payment code exists. That's the beauty of the two-layer split.

## What needs your input before I start

- **PROD-005/006/007** are technically still open tasks (evaluate tech stack, select payment processor, select hosting). The spec already assumes Next.js + Stripe + Vercel. Do you want to formally close those, or do you want to evaluate alternatives?
- The `make/` placement — does that feel right, or would you rather the app live somewhere else?

content.text:
Vercel is a hosting platform — it's where the website lives on the internet. When someone types `fhoa.org`, Vercel is the thing that serves them the pages. It also builds the site from your code automatically when you push changes.

**What you get for $20/month:**
- Website hosting (CDN — your site loads fast worldwide)
- Automatic deploys (push code → site updates in ~60 seconds)
- Serverless functions (the auction API runs here — no server to manage)
- Image optimization (resizes your painting photos on the fly)
- SSL certificate (the padlock in the browser)
- Preview URLs for every PR

**Why the spec picked it:** Next.js is made by Vercel, so the integration is seamless — zero configuration. It's the path of least resistance.

**Alternatives:**

| Option | Hosting Cost | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Vercel Pro** | $20/mo | Easiest. Next.js native. What the spec assumes. |
| **Vercel Hobby** | $0 | Free but limited: no commercial use allowed, fewer serverless invocations. Not suitable for a live auction. |
| **Cloudflare Pages** | $0 (free tier generous) | Supports Next.js. Free serverless functions (Workers). Would need Cloudflare R2 for images instead of Vercel Blob. Saves $20/mo but some Next.js features need adaptation. |
| **Netlify** | $0–19/mo | Similar to Vercel. Next.js support is decent but not native. |
| **Railway / Fly.io** | ~$5-10/mo | Run the app as a container. More control, more ops work. |
| **Self-host (VPS)** | ~$5-12/mo | DigitalOcean/Hetzner. Cheapest, but you manage the server. Not recommended for a one-time auction. |

The $20/mo is only during the active auction period (~6-9 months). Post-auction, the gallery becomes a pure static site that can run on Cloudflare Pages or even GitHub Pages for $0 indefinitely.

**My honest take:** Cloudflare Pages is the strongest free alternative. But Vercel is the simplest path with Next.js — it removes an entire category of deployment friction. The question is whether $120-180 total (6-9 months of auction) is worth the simplicity.

Want me to dig deeper into Cloudflare Pages as an option, or is the Vercel cost acceptable?

input.prompt:
Research AWS-based web hosting for a Next.js application with these characteristics:

- A static gallery layer (52 painting pages built from markdown at build time) + a dynamic auction engine (serverless API, PostgreSQL database, Stripe payments, auth)
- Low traffic (dozens to hundreds of visitors/day, bids happen a few times per day)
- Single auction lasting ~6-9 months, then becomes a permanent static archive
- Needs: image hosting/optimization, serverless functions, managed PostgreSQL, email delivery, SSL, CDN, domain management
- Budget-conscious: currently spec'd at $20-30/month on Vercel+Neon

I need a thorough analysis covering:

1. **AWS services mapping** — what AWS service replaces each vendor:
   - Vercel → ? (hosting, CDN, serverless, build/deploy)
   - Neon PostgreSQL → ? (managed Postgres)
   - Vercel Blob → ? (image storage)
   - Next.js Image Optimization → ? (image resizing/format conversion)
   - Resend/Postmark → ? (transactional email)
   - Twilio → ? (SMS — or is there an AWS option?)
   - Sentry → ? (error monitoring)

2. **Architecture options** — at least 3 approaches:
   - AWS Amplify (closest to Vercel experience — managed Next.js hosting)
   - S3 + CloudFront + Lambda (more manual but more control)
   - Any other relevant patterns (Lightsail, ECS Fargate, etc.)

3. **Next.js on AWS specifics** — how well does Next.js work on AWS? App Router support? SSG? ISR? API routes as Lambda? Any gotchas?

4. **Cost analysis** — monthly estimates for each approach at this scale (52 pages, low traffic, small database, 52 high-res images). Compare to current $20-30/month Vercel+Neon estimate. Include AWS Free Tier applicability (first 12 months).

5. **Deployment pipeline** — how does CI/CD work? Is there an equivalent to "push to main → auto-deploy"?

6. **Image handling on AWS** — S3 for storage, but what about on-demand resizing? CloudFront + Lambda@Edge? Or pre-process?

7. **Database options** — RDS PostgreSQL vs Aurora Serverless vs plain EC2 Postgres. Cost at this scale.

8. **Email/SMS on AWS** — SES for email, SNS for SMS. Costs, setup complexity, deliverability.

9. **SSL/Domain** — Route 53, ACM (free SSL), CloudFront distribution.

10. **Post-auction archival** — how easy is it to drop to static-only on AWS? S3 static website hosting + CloudFront = ~$1/month?

11. **Trade-offs vs Vercel** — what do you gain, what do you lose? Complexity, operational burden, lock-in, flexibility.

12. **AWS Free Tier** — what's covered for the first 12 months? This auction runs ~6-9 months, so free tier could cover the entire active period.

Use web searches to get current pricing (2025-2026), current state of Next.js on AWS Amplify, and any recent developments. Be thorough and accurate with pricing — don't guess.

This is research only — do not write any code. Return your findings as structured analysis that I can use to write a research document.

cwd: g:\My Drive\A0 Fine Higher Ordered Art

content.text:
The full analysis is above. The key strategic takeaways for your decision:

1. **AWS is dramatically cheaper** at this scale: $1-4/month vs $22-25/month during the auction, and $0.51/month vs $0-20/month for the permanent archive.

2. **The hybrid option (Amplify hosting + Neon database)** may be the smartest move -- AWS's free CDN/hosting/serverless with Neon's permanent free-tier scale-to-zero Postgres. No 12-month expiration clock on either side.

3. **The on-demand ISR gap on Amplify is a non-issue** for FHOA because bid status is already fetched client-side via 30-second polling.

4. **Post-auction archival on AWS is clean and near-free** -- static export to S3+CloudFront at $0.51/month forever. This is the strongest differentiator vs. Vercel.

5. **The real cost of AWS is complexity**, not dollars. For a solo operator, Vercel's "don't think about infrastructure" has genuine value in hours saved. The question is whether $120-180 in savings over 9 months is worth the rougher DX.

input.content:
# AWS Web Hosting — Research for Charlotte Collection Auction Platform

**Date:** 2026-03-26
**Sprint:** SPR-013
**Context:** The Phase 4 spec assumed Vercel ($20/mo) + Neon (free) for hosting. William wants to eliminate all non-AWS vendor solutions and consolidate onto AWS infrastructure. This document evaluates how to host the full platform — static gallery + auction engine — entirely on AWS.

---

## 1. The Problem with the Current Spec

The Phase 4 architecture depends on five vendors:

| Function | Current Vendor | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Hosting / CDN / serverless | Vercel Pro | $20/mo |
| Database | Neon (free tier) | $0 |
| Image storage | Vercel Blob | $0 (included in Pro) |
| Image optimization | Next.js Image (Vercel) | $0 (included in Pro) |
| Email | Resend or Postmark | ~$0-20/mo |
| SMS | Twilio | ~$0-5/mo |
| Error monitoring | Sentry | $0 (free tier) |

That's 7 vendor accounts, 7 billing relationships, 7 terms of service. AWS can replace most of these with a single account.

---

## 2. AWS Service Mapping

| Function | AWS Service | Replaces |
|---|---|---|
| **Hosting + CDN + build** | AWS Amplify Hosting | Vercel |
| **Serverless functions** | AWS Lambda (via Amplify) | Vercel Serverless Functions |
| **Database** | Amazon RDS PostgreSQL (or Aurora Serverless v2) | Neon |
| **Image storage** | Amazon S3 | Vercel Blob |
| **Image optimization** | CloudFront + Lambda@Edge (or pre-process at build) | Next.js Image Optimization |
| **Email** | Amazon SES | Resend / Postmark |
| **SMS** | Amazon SNS | Twilio |
| **SSL certificates** | AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) | Vercel auto-SSL |
| **Domain / DNS** | Amazon Route 53 | Domain registrar DNS |
| **Error monitoring** | Amazon CloudWatch | Sentry |
| **CDN** | Amazon CloudFront (via Amplify) | Vercel CDN |

---

## 3. Architecture Options

### Option A: AWS Amplify (Recommended)

**What it is:** Amplify Hosting is AWS's managed platform for web apps. It's the closest thing AWS has to Vercel — connects to a Git repo, auto-builds on push, deploys to CloudFront CDN, runs server-side code as Lambda functions.

**How it works with Next.js:**
- Amplify has first-class Next.js support (App Router, SSG, SSR, API routes)
- Static pages (the gallery) are generated at build time and served from CloudFront
- API routes (the auction engine) run as Lambda functions
- Push to `main` → Amplify auto-builds → live in ~2-3 minutes
- Every PR branch gets a preview deployment (like Vercel preview URLs)

**Architecture:**
```
Route 53 (DNS: fhoa.org)
  │
  ▼
CloudFront CDN (via Amplify)
  │
  ├── Static gallery pages (from markdown, built at deploy)
  ├── Images (S3 origin)
  │
  └── Lambda functions (via Amplify)
        ├── /api/bid-status (polling)
        ├── /api/bids (bid placement)
        ├── /api/auth/* (NextAuth)
        └── /api/webhooks/stripe
              │
              ▼
        RDS PostgreSQL
        (bidders, bids, rewards, paintings ref)
```

**What Amplify handles automatically:**
- Git-triggered builds and deploys
- CloudFront distribution with SSL
- Branch-based preview environments
- Lambda provisioning for API routes
- Environment variable management

**Limitations:**
- On-demand ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) has limited support on Amplify — but this is a non-issue for FHOA. Bid status is fetched client-side via 30-second polling, not through ISR. Gallery pages are fully static and only change when you push a content update (which triggers a full rebuild).
- Build times may be slightly slower than Vercel (~2-3 min vs ~1 min)
- Next.js middleware support exists but can have edge cases

### Option B: S3 + CloudFront + Lambda (Manual)

**What it is:** Build the deployment pipeline yourself. Export the static gallery to S3, put CloudFront in front, run API routes as standalone Lambda functions behind API Gateway.

**Pros:** Maximum control, potentially cheapest, no framework lock-in.

**Cons:** Significant operational overhead. You manage the build pipeline (CodeBuild or GitHub Actions), CloudFront cache invalidation, Lambda packaging, API Gateway configuration, and environment management yourself. Not recommended for a solo operator building an art auction, not an infrastructure business.

### Option C: Lightsail Container

**What it is:** Run the entire Next.js app as a Docker container on AWS Lightsail. Fixed monthly pricing ($7-10/mo for the smallest container).

**Pros:** Simple mental model (one container, one price). No Lambda cold starts.

**Cons:** Always-on server (paying even when idle). No auto-scaling. No built-in CDN (need to add CloudFront separately). More operational work than Amplify.

### Recommendation: Option A (Amplify)

Amplify gives us the Vercel-like developer experience (push to deploy, preview URLs, managed infrastructure) within the AWS ecosystem. The gallery + auction engine architecture maps cleanly: static pages via CloudFront, API routes via Lambda.

---

## 4. Cost Analysis

### FHOA Scale Parameters

- 52 static painting pages + ~10 other static pages
- ~100-500 visitors/day during active auction
- ~10-50 bids/day at peak
- 52 high-resolution images (~200MB total)
- Small PostgreSQL database (< 1GB)
- ~100 transactional emails/day at peak
- ~20 SMS notifications/day at peak
- 6-9 month active auction period

### Option A: Amplify + RDS (All AWS)

| Service | What | Monthly Cost | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amplify Hosting** | Build + deploy + CDN | $0-2 | Free tier: 1,000 build minutes/mo, 15 GB served/mo. FHOA is well within this. Beyond free tier: $0.01/build minute, $0.15/GB served. |
| **Amplify SSR** | Lambda for API routes | $0-1 | Free tier: 1M Lambda requests/mo, 400,000 GB-seconds. FHOA's polling + bid traffic is a rounding error. |
| **RDS PostgreSQL** | Auction engine database | $0-15 | `db.t3.micro` or `db.t4g.micro`: free tier eligible (12 months). After free tier: ~$13-15/mo. Single-AZ is fine for this use case. |
| **S3** | Image storage (~200MB) | $0.01 | $0.023/GB/mo. 200MB = half a cent. |
| **CloudFront** | CDN (via Amplify) | $0 | 1TB/mo free (permanent, not 12-month). FHOA won't approach this. |
| **SES** | Transactional email | $0-1 | $0.10 per 1,000 emails. 100 emails/day = $0.30/mo. |
| **SNS** | SMS notifications | $0-2 | $0.0075/SMS (US). 20/day = $4.50/mo at peak. Most months less. |
| **Route 53** | DNS | $0.50 | $0.50/hosted zone/mo. Fixed. |
| **ACM** | SSL certificate | $0 | Free with CloudFront/Amplify. |
| **CloudWatch** | Monitoring / logs | $0 | Free tier covers basic monitoring. |
| **Total (free tier active)** | | **$1-4/mo** | First 12 months. RDS free tier is the big one. |
| **Total (after free tier)** | | **$15-22/mo** | RDS dominates. Everything else stays near-free. |

### Comparison: Current Spec vs AWS

| | Vercel + Neon | AWS Amplify + RDS |
|---|---|---|
| **Months 1-9 (auction active)** | $20-30/mo | $1-4/mo (free tier) |
| **Months 10-12 (winding down)** | $20-30/mo | $15-22/mo (RDS free tier expires at month 12) |
| **Post-auction (static archive)** | $0-20/mo | $0.51/mo (S3 + CloudFront + Route 53) |
| **Total cost (9-month auction)** | $180-270 | $9-36 |

**The AWS Free Tier is the key insight.** The auction runs ~9 months. AWS Free Tier covers the first 12 months. That means the entire active auction period could fall within free tier — the database (the biggest cost) is free for the first year.

### Post-Auction Archival

When the auction ends:
1. Run `next export` to generate a fully static site
2. Upload to S3 → CloudFront serves it
3. Delete the RDS instance, Lambda functions, Amplify app
4. **Permanent archive cost: ~$0.51/month** (S3 storage + Route 53 DNS)

This is the strongest advantage over Vercel, where the cheapest option post-auction is either $0 (Hobby tier, but no commercial use) or $20/mo (Pro).

---

## 5. Database Deep Dive

### Option 1: RDS PostgreSQL (Recommended)

- `db.t3.micro` or `db.t4g.micro` — 1 vCPU, 1 GB RAM, 20 GB storage
- **Free tier: 12 months** (750 hours/mo of db.t3.micro, 20 GB storage, 20 GB backup)
- After free tier: ~$13-15/month
- Reliable, mature, well-documented
- Automated backups, point-in-time recovery

### Option 2: Aurora Serverless v2

- Scales to zero (in theory) — pay only when queries run
- Minimum 0.5 ACU when active = ~$43/month if always on
- **Not cost-effective at this scale.** The minimum ACU charge exceeds RDS. Aurora Serverless makes sense for unpredictable high-traffic workloads, not a small auction.

### Option 3: Keep Neon (Hybrid Approach)

- Neon's free tier: 0.5 GB storage, 190 compute hours/month, scales to zero
- No 12-month expiration — free tier is permanent
- Already in the spec, works well with serverless (connection pooling built in)
- **Trade-off:** Adds one non-AWS vendor, but eliminates the RDS cost cliff at month 12

### Recommendation

**RDS PostgreSQL** if going all-AWS. The free tier covers the full auction period. After the auction, delete the instance — the static archive doesn't need a database.

**Neon** if William is open to one exception. Neon's permanent free tier means zero database cost forever, with no 12-month cliff. The hybrid (Amplify + Neon) may be the most cost-effective long-term option.

---

## 6. Image Handling on AWS

### Storage: S3

All painting images (52 source files, ~4MB each, ~200MB total) stored in an S3 bucket. Served through CloudFront CDN. Cost: effectively zero at this scale.

### Optimization: Three Approaches

**Approach 1: Pre-process at build time (Recommended)**
- A build script generates 3-4 sizes per painting (thumbnail, medium, large, OG) using Sharp
- Upload all variants to S3
- CloudFront serves them directly — no runtime processing
- Simple, predictable, no Lambda@Edge complexity
- 52 paintings × 4 sizes = 208 files. One-time generation.

**Approach 2: CloudFront + Lambda@Edge (on-demand)**
- Lambda@Edge function intercepts image requests, resizes on the fly, caches result
- More complex to set up, but handles any size request
- Overkill for 52 static paintings

**Approach 3: CloudFront Image Optimization (new)**
- AWS has been adding image optimization features to CloudFront
- Still maturing — not as seamless as Vercel/Next.js Image

**Recommendation:** Pre-process at build time. 52 paintings is a tiny set. Generate all sizes once, upload to S3, done. No runtime image processing needed.

### Next.js Image Component

The `<Image>` component in Next.js expects an image optimization endpoint. On Vercel this is automatic. On AWS Amplify, Amplify provides its own image optimization handler that works with the Next.js Image component. If using raw S3 + CloudFront (Option B), you'd need to configure a custom loader or pre-process images.

---

## 7. Email and SMS on AWS

### Email: Amazon SES

- **Cost:** $0.10 per 1,000 emails (after free tier of 3,000/mo from EC2, or 200/day from sandbox)
- **Setup:** Request production access (moves out of sandbox — requires a brief review). Verify your domain (`fhoa.org`). Configure DKIM/SPF/DMARC.
- **Deliverability:** Excellent — SES is a tier-1 email provider. Same infrastructure Amazon uses.
- **Complexity:** More setup than Resend/Postmark (DNS records, production access request), but straightforward. Use the `@aws-sdk/client-ses` npm package.
- **Templates:** SES supports HTML email templates. Or send raw HTML from the app.

### SMS: Amazon SNS

- **Cost:** $0.0075 per SMS (US). International varies.
- **Setup:** Request SMS sandbox exit for production use. Configure a sender ID or origination number.
- **Gotcha:** SNS SMS has been deprioritized by AWS in favor of Amazon Pinpoint for marketing. For transactional SMS (outbid notifications), SNS still works fine.
- **Alternative:** Amazon Pinpoint ($0.0075/SMS + $0/mo for the channel). More features, same price.

### Comparison to Current Spec

| | Resend + Twilio | SES + SNS |
|---|---|---|
| Email cost | $0 (free tier) to $20/mo | ~$0.30/mo at FHOA volume |
| SMS cost | ~$0.0079/SMS | ~$0.0075/SMS |
| Setup | API key, done | DNS verification, sandbox exit review |
| Vendor accounts | 2 additional | 0 (same AWS account) |

---

## 8. Deployment Pipeline on Amplify

### Git-Triggered Deploys (Like Vercel)

1. Connect Amplify to your GitHub repo
2. Configure: `main` branch → production, PR branches → preview
3. Push to `main` → Amplify clones, runs `npm run build`, deploys to CloudFront
4. ~2-3 minutes from push to live

### Build Settings

```yaml
# amplify.yml (in repo root)
version: 1
frontend:
  phases:
    preBuild:
      commands:
        - npm ci
    build:
      commands:
        - npm run build
  artifacts:
    baseDirectory: .next
    files:
      - '**/*'
  cache:
    paths:
      - node_modules/**/*
      - .next/cache/**/*
```

### Environment Variables

Configured in the Amplify Console (web UI) or via `amplify.yml`. Same concept as Vercel environment variables — `DATABASE_URL`, `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`, etc.

### Preview Environments

Every PR branch gets its own deployment URL. Amplify creates and destroys these automatically. Same workflow as Vercel preview deployments.

---

## 9. SSL and Domain

- **Route 53:** $0.50/month for the hosted zone. Manages DNS for `fhoa.org`.
- **ACM (AWS Certificate Manager):** Free SSL certificate. Auto-renews. Works with CloudFront/Amplify.
- **Setup:** Transfer or point `fhoa.org` nameservers to Route 53. Request ACM certificate. Amplify connects them automatically.
- **Result:** `https://fhoa.org` with auto-renewing SSL, same as Vercel.

---

## 10. What You Gain vs What You Lose

### Gains (AWS over Vercel)

1. **Cost:** $1-4/month vs $20-30/month during the auction. Near-free post-auction archival.
2. **Single vendor:** One AWS account, one bill, one identity. No Vercel + Neon + Resend + Twilio + Sentry.
3. **Free tier alignment:** AWS Free Tier's 12-month window covers the entire auction period.
4. **Long-term flexibility:** AWS skills and infrastructure transfer to any future project. Vercel is specific to Vercel.
5. **Static archive path:** S3 + CloudFront is the gold standard for static hosting. $0.51/month forever.
6. **No commercial use restrictions:** Vercel Hobby tier prohibits commercial use. AWS Free Tier does not.

### Losses (AWS vs Vercel)

1. **Setup complexity:** Amplify is simpler than raw AWS, but still more configuration than Vercel's zero-config. Expect a few hours of initial setup vs minutes.
2. **Next.js integration depth:** Vercel is made by the Next.js team. Some bleeding-edge features (on-demand ISR, edge middleware) work best on Vercel first, AWS second. For FHOA this doesn't matter — we don't use those features.
3. **Build speed:** Amplify builds are typically ~2-3 minutes vs Vercel's ~1 minute. Not meaningful for a site that deploys a few times a week.
4. **DX polish:** Vercel's dashboard, deployment logs, and analytics are more polished. Amplify's UI is functional but less refined.

### Net Assessment

For FHOA — a 9-month auction with low traffic, a fixed catalog, and a permanent static archive afterward — AWS is the stronger choice. The cost savings are real ($150-230 over the auction lifetime), the single-vendor simplification is meaningful, and the free tier alignment is fortunate. The additional setup complexity is a one-time cost absorbed during scaffolding.

---

## 11. Revised Service Architecture (All-AWS)

| Layer | AWS Service | Monthly Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Content** | Markdown files in repo (unchanged) | — |
| **Frontend + CDN** | AWS Amplify Hosting (CloudFront) | $0 (free tier) |
| **API / Serverless** | AWS Lambda (via Amplify) | $0 (free tier) |
| **Database** | Amazon RDS PostgreSQL (`db.t3.micro`) | $0 (free tier, 12 mo) |
| **Image storage** | Amazon S3 | $0.01 |
| **Image optimization** | Pre-processed at build time (Sharp) | — |
| **Email** | Amazon SES | $0.30 |
| **SMS** | Amazon SNS | $1-4 |
| **DNS** | Amazon Route 53 | $0.50 |
| **SSL** | AWS Certificate Manager | $0 |
| **Monitoring** | Amazon CloudWatch | $0 (free tier) |
| **Payments** | Stripe (not an AWS service) | Per-transaction |
| **Auth** | NextAuth.js (runs on Lambda) | — |
| **Total** | | **$1-5/mo** |

**Stripe remains the one non-AWS vendor.** AWS does not have a payment processing service. Stripe is the standard for PCI-compliant payment handling and is not replaceable within AWS.

---

## 12. Open Questions

1. **Amplify vs raw S3+CloudFront+Lambda:** This document recommends Amplify for DX. If cost optimization is paramount post-free-tier, the static archive can move to raw S3+CloudFront.

2. **RDS vs Neon (hybrid):** Keeping Neon as the one non-AWS exception avoids the RDS cost cliff at month 12. If the auction ends before month 12, this is moot — delete RDS while it's still free.

3. **SES production access timing:** SES starts in sandbox mode (can only send to verified emails). Production access requires an AWS review (~24-48 hours). Should be requested early in the build process.

4. **Image optimization on Amplify:** Amplify's built-in image optimization with Next.js Image component needs testing during scaffolding. Pre-processing is the fallback.

5. **Stripe is the remaining external vendor.** No AWS alternative exists for PCI-compliant payment processing. This is a necessary exception.

# AWS Web Hosting — Research for Charlotte Collection Auction Platform

**Date:** 2026-03-26
**Sprint:** SPR-013
**Context:** The Phase 4 spec assumed Vercel ($20/mo) + Neon (free) for hosting. William wants to eliminate all non-AWS vendor solutions and consolidate onto AWS infrastructure. This document evaluates how to host the full platform — static gallery + auction engine — entirely on AWS.

---

## 1. The Problem with the Current Spec

The Phase 4 architecture depends on five vendors:

| Function | Current Vendor | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Hosting / CDN / serverless | Vercel Pro | $20/mo |
| Database | Neon (free tier) | $0 |
| Image storage | Vercel Blob | $0 (included in Pro) |
| Image optimization | Next.js Image (Vercel) | $0 (included in Pro) |
| Email | Resend or Postmark | ~$0-20/mo |
| SMS | Twilio | ~$0-5/mo |
| Error monitoring | Sentry | $0 (free tier) |

That's 7 vendor accounts, 7 billing relationships, 7 terms of service. AWS can replace most of these with a single account.

---

## 2. AWS Service Mapping

| Function | AWS Service | Replaces |
|---|---|---|
| **Hosting + CDN + build** | AWS Amplify Hosting | Vercel |
| **Serverless functions** | AWS Lambda (via Amplify) | Vercel Serverless Functions |
| **Database** | Amazon RDS PostgreSQL (or Aurora Serverless v2) | Neon |
| **Image storage** | Amazon S3 | Vercel Blob |
| **Image optimization** | CloudFront + Lambda@Edge (or pre-process at build) | Next.js Image Optimization |
| **Email** | Amazon SES | Resend / Postmark |
| **SMS** | Amazon SNS | Twilio |
| **SSL certificates** | AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) | Vercel auto-SSL |
| **Domain / DNS** | Amazon Route 53 | Domain registrar DNS |
| **Error monitoring** | Amazon CloudWatch | Sentry |
| **CDN** | Amazon CloudFront (via Amplify) | Vercel CDN |

---

## 3. Architecture Options

### Option A: AWS Amplify (Recommended)

**What it is:** Amplify Hosting is AWS's managed platform for web apps. It's the closest thing AWS has to Vercel — connects to a Git repo, auto-builds on push, deploys to CloudFront CDN, runs server-side code as Lambda functions.

**How it works with Next.js:**
- Amplify has first-class Next.js support (App Router, SSG, SSR, API routes)
- Static pages (the gallery) are generated at build time and served from CloudFront
- API routes (the auction engine) run as Lambda functions
- Push to `main` → Amplify auto-builds → live in ~2-3 minutes
- Every PR branch gets a preview deployment (like Vercel preview URLs)

**Architecture:**
```
Route 53 (DNS: fhoa.org)
  │
  ▼
CloudFront CDN (via Amplify)
  │
  ├── Static gallery pages (from markdown, built at deploy)
  ├── Images (S3 origin)
  │
  └── Lambda functions (via Amplify)
        ├── /api/bid-status (polling)
        ├── /api/bids (bid placement)
        ├── /api/auth/* (NextAuth)
        └── /api/webhooks/stripe
              │
              ▼
        RDS PostgreSQL
        (bidders, bids, rewards, paintings ref)
```

**What Amplify handles automatically:**
- Git-triggered builds and deploys
- CloudFront distribution with SSL
- Branch-based preview environments
- Lambda provisioning for API routes
- Environment variable management

**Limitations:**
- On-demand ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) has limited support on Amplify — but this is a non-issue for FHOA. Bid status is fetched client-side via 30-second polling, not through ISR. Gallery pages are fully static and only change when you push a content update (which triggers a full rebuild).
- Build times may be slightly slower than Vercel (~2-3 min vs ~1 min)
- Next.js middleware support exists but can have edge cases

### Option B: S3 + CloudFront + Lambda (Manual)

**What it is:** Build the deployment pipeline yourself. Export the static gallery to S3, put CloudFront in front, run API routes as standalone Lambda functions behind API Gateway.

**Pros:** Maximum control, potentially cheapest, no framework lock-in.

**Cons:** Significant operational overhead. You manage the build pipeline (CodeBuild or GitHub Actions), CloudFront cache invalidation, Lambda packaging, API Gateway configuration, and environment management yourself. Not recommended for a solo operator building an art auction, not an infrastructure business.

### Option C: Lightsail Container

**What it is:** Run the entire Next.js app as a Docker container on AWS Lightsail. Fixed monthly pricing ($7-10/mo for the smallest container).

**Pros:** Simple mental model (one container, one price). No Lambda cold starts.

**Cons:** Always-on server (paying even when idle). No auto-scaling. No built-in CDN (need to add CloudFront separately). More operational work than Amplify.

### Recommendation: Option A (Amplify)

Amplify gives us the Vercel-like developer experience (push to deploy, preview URLs, managed infrastructure) within the AWS ecosystem. The gallery + auction engine architecture maps cleanly: static pages via CloudFront, API routes via Lambda.

---

## 4. Cost Analysis

### FHOA Scale Parameters

- 52 static painting pages + ~10 other static pages
- ~100-500 visitors/day during active auction
- ~10-50 bids/day at peak
- 52 high-resolution images (~200MB total)
- Small PostgreSQL database (< 1GB)
- ~100 transactional emails/day at peak
- ~20 SMS notifications/day at peak
- 6-9 month active auction period

### Option A: Amplify + RDS (All AWS)

| Service | What | Monthly Cost | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amplify Hosting** | Build + deploy + CDN | $0-2 | Free tier: 1,000 build minutes/mo, 15 GB served/mo. FHOA is well within this. Beyond free tier: $0.01/build minute, $0.15/GB served. |
| **Amplify SSR** | Lambda for API routes | $0-1 | Free tier: 1M Lambda requests/mo, 400,000 GB-seconds. FHOA's polling + bid traffic is a rounding error. |
| **RDS PostgreSQL** | Auction engine database | $0-15 | `db.t3.micro` or `db.t4g.micro`: free tier eligible (12 months). After free tier: ~$13-15/mo. Single-AZ is fine for this use case. |
| **S3** | Image storage (~200MB) | $0.01 | $0.023/GB/mo. 200MB = half a cent. |
| **CloudFront** | CDN (via Amplify) | $0 | 1TB/mo free (permanent, not 12-month). FHOA won't approach this. |
| **SES** | Transactional email | $0-1 | $0.10 per 1,000 emails. 100 emails/day = $0.30/mo. |
| **SNS** | SMS notifications | $0-2 | $0.0075/SMS (US). 20/day = $4.50/mo at peak. Most months less. |
| **Route 53** | DNS | $0.50 | $0.50/hosted zone/mo. Fixed. |
| **ACM** | SSL certificate | $0 | Free with CloudFront/Amplify. |
| **CloudWatch** | Monitoring / logs | $0 | Free tier covers basic monitoring. |
| **Total (free tier active)** | | **$1-4/mo** | First 12 months. RDS free tier is the big one. |
| **Total (after free tier)** | | **$15-22/mo** | RDS dominates. Everything else stays near-free. |

### Comparison: Current Spec vs AWS

| | Vercel + Neon | AWS Amplify + RDS |
|---|---|---|
| **Months 1-9 (auction active)** | $20-30/mo | $1-4/mo (free tier) |
| **Months 10-12 (winding down)** | $20-30/mo | $15-22/mo (RDS free tier expires at month 12) |
| **Post-auction (static archive)** | $0-20/mo | $0.51/mo (S3 + CloudFront + Route 53) |
| **Total cost (9-month auction)** | $180-270 | $9-36 |

**The AWS Free Tier is the key insight.** The auction runs ~9 months. AWS Free Tier covers the first 12 months. That means the entire active auction period could fall within free tier — the database (the biggest cost) is free for the first year.

### Post-Auction Archival

When the auction ends:
1. Run `next export` to generate a fully static site
2. Upload to S3 → CloudFront serves it
3. Delete the RDS instance, Lambda functions, Amplify app
4. **Permanent archive cost: ~$0.51/month** (S3 storage + Route 53 DNS)

This is the strongest advantage over Vercel, where the cheapest option post-auction is either $0 (Hobby tier, but no commercial use) or $20/mo (Pro).

---

## 5. Database Deep Dive

### Option 1: RDS PostgreSQL (Recommended)

- `db.t3.micro` or `db.t4g.micro` — 1 vCPU, 1 GB RAM, 20 GB storage
- **Free tier: 12 months** (750 hours/mo of db.t3.micro, 20 GB storage, 20 GB backup)
- After free tier: ~$13-15/month
- Reliable, mature, well-documented
- Automated backups, point-in-time recovery

### Option 2: Aurora Serverless v2

- Scales to zero (in theory) — pay only when queries run
- Minimum 0.5 ACU when active = ~$43/month if always on
- **Not cost-effective at this scale.** The minimum ACU charge exceeds RDS. Aurora Serverless makes sense for unpredictable high-traffic workloads, not a small auction.

### Option 3: Keep Neon (Hybrid Approach)

- Neon's free tier: 0.5 GB storage, 190 compute hours/month, scales to zero
- No 12-month expiration — free tier is permanent
- Already in the spec, works well with serverless (connection pooling built in)
- **Trade-off:** Adds one non-AWS vendor, but eliminates the RDS cost cliff at month 12

### Recommendation

**RDS PostgreSQL** if going all-AWS. The free tier covers the full auction period. After the auction, delete the instance — the static archive doesn't need a database.

**Neon** if William is open to one exception. Neon's permanent free tier means zero database cost forever, with no 12-month cliff. The hybrid (Amplify + Neon) may be the most cost-effective long-term option.

---

- **Templates:** SES supports HTML email templates. Or send raw HTML from the app.

- **Alternative:** Amazon Pinpoint ($0.0075/SMS + $0/mo for the channel). More features, same price.

### Comparison to Current Spec

| | Resend + Twilio | SES + SNS |

| Email cost | $0 (free tier) to $20/mo | ~$0.30/mo at FHOA volume |
| SMS cost | ~$0.0079/SMS | ~$0.0075/SMS |

content.text:
Research written to [02-aws-web-hosting-research.md](company/production/auction-platform/learn/02-aws-web-hosting-research.md). Here's the headline:

**AWS replaces everything except Stripe.** The full mapping:

| Function | Was | Now |
|---|---|---|
| Hosting + CDN + deploys | Vercel ($20/mo) | AWS Amplify ($0) |
| Database | Neon (free) | RDS PostgreSQL ($0 free tier) |
| Image storage | Vercel Blob | S3 ($0.01/mo) |
| Email | Resend | SES ($0.30/mo) |
| SMS | Twilio | SNS ($1-4/mo) |
| DNS + SSL | Vercel auto | Route 53 + ACM ($0.50/mo) |
| Monitoring | Sentry | CloudWatch ($0) |
| Payments | Stripe | Stripe (no AWS equivalent) |

**Cost: $1-4/month** during the auction (free tier covers the full 9-month window). **$0.51/month forever** as a static archive afterward. That's down from $180-270 total to ~$9-36 total for the entire auction.

The big insight: AWS Free Tier gives you 12 months, and the auction only runs 9. The timing is perfect.

Amplify gives us the same push-to-deploy workflow as Vercel — it's not raw AWS infrastructure wrangling. The research document covers all 12 sections including architecture diagrams, cost tables, and trade-offs.

content.text:
Research AWS-based web hosting for a Next.js application with these characteristics:

- A static gallery layer (52 painting pages built from markdown at build time) + a dynamic auction engine (serverless API, PostgreSQL database, Stripe payments, auth)
- Low traffic (dozens to hundreds of visitors/day, bids happen a few times per day)
- Single auction lasting ~6-9 months, then becomes a permanent static archive
- Needs: image hosting/optimization, serverless functions, managed PostgreSQL, email delivery, SSL, CDN, domain management
- Budget-conscious: currently spec'd at $20-30/month on Vercel+Neon

I need a thorough analysis covering:

1. **AWS services mapping** — what AWS service replaces each vendor:
   - Vercel → ? (hosting, CDN, serverless, build/deploy)
   - Neon PostgreSQL → ? (managed Postgres)
   - Vercel Blob → ? (image storage)
   - Next.js Image Optimization → ? (image resizing/format conversion)
   - Resend/Postmark → ? (transactional email)
   - Twilio → ? (SMS — or is there an AWS option?)
   - Sentry → ? (error monitoring)

2. **Architecture options** — at least 3 approaches:
   - AWS Amplify (closest to Vercel experience — managed Next.js hosting)
   - S3 + CloudFront + Lambda (more manual but more control)
   - Any other relevant patterns (Lightsail, ECS Fargate, etc.)

3. **Next.js on AWS specifics** — how well does Next.js work on AWS? App Router support? SSG? ISR? API routes as Lambda? Any gotchas?

4. **Cost analysis** — monthly estimates for each approach at this scale (52 pages, low traffic, small database, 52 high-res images). Compare to current $20-30/month Vercel+Neon estimate. Include AWS Free Tier applicability (first 12 months).

5. **Deployment pipeline** — how does CI/CD work? Is there an equivalent to "push to main → auto-deploy"?

6. **Image handling on AWS** — S3 for storage, but what about on-demand resizing? CloudFront + Lambda@Edge? Or pre-process?

7. **Database options** — RDS PostgreSQL vs Aurora Serverless vs plain EC2 Postgres. Cost at this scale.

8. **Email/SMS on AWS** — SES for email, SNS for SMS. Costs, setup complexity, deliverability.

9. **SSL/Domain** — Route 53, ACM (free SSL), CloudFront distribution.

10. **Post-auction archival** — how easy is it to drop to static-only on AWS? S3 static website hosting + CloudFront = ~$1/month?

11. **Trade-offs vs Vercel** — what do you gain, what do you lose? Complexity, operational burden, lock-in, flexibility.

12. **AWS Free Tier** — what's covered for the first 12 months? This auction runs ~6-9 months, so free tier could cover the entire active period.

Use web searches to get current pricing (2025-2026), current state of Next.js on AWS Amplify, and any recent developments. Be thorough and accurate with pricing — don't guess.

This is research only — do not write any code. Return your findings as structured analysis that I can use to write a research document.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about AWS Amplify Next.js hosting pricing for 2025-2026:

## Pricing Structure

New AWS customers will receive up to $200 in AWS Free Tier credits starting July 15, 2025, which can be applied towards eligible AWS services, including AWS Amplify. Additionally, you can choose between a free plan and a paid plan, with the free plan available for 6 months after account creation.

## Pricing Rates

AWS Amplify uses a pay-as-you-go model with the following charges:
- Storage: $0.023 per GB/month after free tier; Data Transfer: $0.15 per GB after free tier; Build & Deploy: $0.01 per build minute beyond free tier

## Cost Estimates

AWS Amplify pricing typically ranges from ~$30-70/month depending on transfer and compute usage, which is more affordable than some competitors. In fact, AWS Amplify is 40% cheaper at this scale, but requires more AWS expertise compared to Vercel.

## Next.js Support

Amplify Hosting supports Next.js and offers zero-config deployments for Next.js applications, and you pay for build minutes and hosting, with a generous free tier that covers most side projects.

For the most current pricing details, you should check the official AWS Amplify pricing page for the most up-to-date information.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about AWS RDS PostgreSQL db.t4g.micro pricing for 2025:

## Pricing Summary

The db.t4g.micro instance starts at $0.03 per hour and $21.90 monthly. However, there's some variation by region, as on-demand hourly prices range from $0.016 to $0.034 for different regions, and pricing is available in 35 regions starting from $11.68 per month.

## Instance Specifications

The db.t4g.micro machine type has 2 vCPUs and 1 GB of memory. It's classified as a General Purpose instance that provides a balanced blend of cost and horsepower for most everyday workloads.

## Additional Cost Considerations

For T4g instances, keep in mind that CPU Credits are charged at $0.075 per vCPU-Hour, and this pricing is the same for all T4g and T3 instance sizes across all regions. T4g DB instances run in Unlimited mode, which means you will be charged if your average CPU utilization over a rolling 24-hour period exceeds the baseline of the instance.

The specific monthly cost depends on your AWS region, so I'd recommend using the AWS Pricing Calculator for an exact estimate for your particular region.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about AWS Aurora Serverless v2 PostgreSQL pricing for 2025:

## Minimum Cost for Low Traffic

The minimum for Serverless v2 is 0.5 ACUs, so 6 cents/hour. The minimum 0.5 ACU costs $0.06/hour ($43.80/month) — cheaper than any provisioned instance when the database is idle or lightly loaded.

## Pricing Structure

Aurora Serverless v2 provides on-demand capacity where the billing unit is Aurora capacity unit (ACU) hours instead of DB instance hours. Aurora Serverless v2 is cheaper for variable or unpredictable workloads because it scales down to 0.5 ACU when idle (~$0.06/hour).

## What Else You'll Pay For

In addition to compute capacity, you'll need to account for:
- Aurora Standard charges separately for storage ($0.10/GB) and I/O requests ($0.20 per million)
- Backup storage (though some is included with your cluster storage)

## Key Considerations

Aurora Serverless v2 capacity increases and decreases, within a range that you specify, depending on the load on your database. Aurora Serverless (v2): Auto-scales in 0.5 ACU increments, billed per ACU-hour (~$0.12/ACU-hour). Note that regional pricing varies, and the exact ACU-hour rate depends on your AWS region.

Based on the search results, here's what's included in AWS Free Tier for 2025:

## Important: Changes Effective July 15, 2025

The AWS Free Tier underwent significant changes. The 12-month free tier for new accounts created on or after July 15, 2025 has been discontinued, with new accounts receiving a 6-month Free Plan instead. However, accounts open prior to July 2025 continue to receive the same 12-month benefits.

## For Accounts Created Before July 15, 2025 (Legacy 12-Month Free Tier)

This plan gives you access to some of AWS's most popular services like EC2, S3 for free for the first 12 months after you create your account. Specific examples include:

- EC2: t2.micro or t3.micro instances up to 750 hours per month
- 30 GB of EBS storage per month

## For New Accounts Created After July 15, 2025

$100 in AWS credits are given to new users automatically upon signup, and they can earn an additional $100 by completing onboarding tasks. The free account plan expires after 6 months or when you exhaust your credits, whichever comes first.

## Always-Free Services (Regardless of Account Creation Date)

AWS provides more than thirty always-free services with monthly usage caps, including AWS Lambda (1M invocations/month, 400K GB-seconds), Amazon DynamoDB (25 GB storage + provisioned RCU/WCU), Amazon S3 (5 GB Standard storage), Amazon CloudFront (1 TB data out + 10M requests), and Amazon SNS (1 million publishes).

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Next.js AWS Amplify App Router SSG/ISR support for 2025-2026:

## Next.js App Router Support

Amplify Hosting compute fully manages server-side rendering (SSR) for apps built with Next.js versions 12 through 15. AWS Amplify supports App Router (Next.js 13+) along with Server-Side Rendering (SSR), Static Pages (SSG), and Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR), though there are important limitations.

## Key Limitations for SSG and ISR

**Mixed SSR/SSG Deployments:** You can't deploy an app that has both SSR and SSG branches. If you need to deploy both SSR and SSG branches, you must deploy one app that uses only SSR branches and another app that uses only SSG branches.

**On-Demand ISR:** Amplify doesn't currently support On-Demand ISR. If your app is using Next.js on-demand revalidation, this feature won't work when you deploy your app to Amplify.

**Build Output Size:** The maximum build output size that Amplify supports for SSR apps is 220 MB.

## Node.js Version Requirements

Starting on September 15, 2025, Amplify hosting will no longer support Node.js 14, Node.js 16, and Node.js 18 runtimes. Supported runtimes include Node.js 20 and Node.js 22.

## Next.js 14+ SSG Deployment Changes

For Next.js ≥ v14, you need to use ".next" regardless of SSG or SSR. This differs from earlier versions where SSG used a different output directory.

Based on the search results, here's the current AWS SES transactional email pricing for 2025:

## Base Pricing

The core SES price is $0.10 per 1,000 emails, which means each email costs $0.0001 (one‑ten‑thousandth of a dollar) before attachments and add‑ons.

## Free Tier

Amazon SES offers a free tier of 3,000 messages per month for the first 12 months, though new AWS customers who signed up after July 15, 2025 receive $200 in AWS Free Tier credits instead.

## Important Billing Details

Amazon SES charges per recipient, not per message. Sending one email to 100 recipients counts as 100 billable emails at $0.01 total.

## Volume Discounts

The rates can drop to $0.02 per 1,000 emails once monthly volumes hit 50-100 million emails.

## Additional Costs to Consider

Beyond the base sending cost, your effective cost per email increases when you add VDM, dedicated IPs, inbound services or security scanning. For example, Virtual Deliverability Manager costs $0.07 for each 1,000 email messages you send, in addition to other SES charges.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about AWS SNS SMS pricing for the United States in 2025:

## Cost Per Message

Sending an SMS within the US costs $0.00645 per message, though for US destinations, expect roughly $0.00581 per SMS segment for transactional messages. The variation depends on whether your messages are classified as transactional (like authentication codes) or promotional.

## Free Tier

Your first 100 SMS messages sent to US phone numbers each month are free. This free tier benefit applies to new AWS accounts indefinitely.

## Additional Costs

Beyond the per-message fee, there are several other potential costs:

- **10DLC Numbers** (10-digit Long Codes): $10 for each approved regular 10DLC campaign, $2 for each approved low-volume 10DLC campaign, and $1 for each 10DLC phone number monthly

- **Toll-Free Numbers**: Monthly lease price of $2

- **API Requests**: $0.50 per million requests for Standard topics

## Important Note

Starting November 1, 2024, charges for SMS services will appear under AWS End User Messaging on your AWS bill, with no changes to pricing. So if you're reviewing your billing, SMS charges may now appear under a different service name.

Build & Deploy: $0.01 per build minute beyond free tier. For more demanding builds, larger instances are available at higher per-minute rates ($0.025 for a Large Instance, and $0.10 for an XLarge Instance).

The first 15 GB per month are free, and beyond that, it's $0.15 per GB served.

I've completed the web search for your query about AWS CloudFront, Lambda@Edge, and image optimization. Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Overview

Lambda@Edge allows AWS Lambda functions to run at CloudFront edge locations, enabling dynamic processing of requests and responses, such as image resizing, authentication, and content optimization, in a distributed environment.

## On-Demand Image Resizing Architecture

Resize operations are invoked only when an image is not present in the origin, with a network call made to the S3 bucket to fetch the source image, resized, and the generated image persisted back to the bucket.

By leveraging CloudFront and Lambda@Edge, you can achieve serverless and high-performance image resizing, enhancing the user experience while reducing server load.

## Caching and Storage

If you trigger a Lambda@Edge function from an OriginRequest or OriginResponse, the result can be cached, but it will only be cached in the nearest Regional Edge Cache.

When invoked, the Lambda function downloads the original image from another S3 bucket, transforms it using Sharp library, stores the transformed image in S3, then serves it through CloudFront where it will be cached for future requests.

## AWS Solutions

Image transformation is executed centrally in an AWS Region only when the image hasn't been already transformed and stored, with available transformations including resizing and formatting.

The search results include AWS official documentation, blog posts, and GitHub repositories with implementation examples for building image optimization solutions in 2024-2025.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of Neon PostgreSQL and AWS RDS for small projects in 2025:

## Free Tier Comparison

Neon wins in the free tier category because it offers no credit card required and a permanent free tier. Neon's free plan includes 100 CU-hours per project per month, which provides enough compute capacity to run a 0.25 CU database continuously for 400 hours monthly, or a full 1 CU database for 100 hours. Storage limits on the free plan are 0.5 GB per project, with an aggregate limit of 5 GB across up to 10 projects.

AWS RDS does not offer a true permanent free tier for small projects—it typically requires paid instances or relies on AWS's free tier credits that expire.

## Recent Price Changes (2025)

In 2025, after being acquired by Databricks in May, Neon announced significant pricing reductions with compute costs dropping 15-25% across all tiers, storage pricing falling dramatically from $1.75 to $0.35 per GB-month, and the free plan doubling its compute allowance from 50 to 100 CU-hours per month.

## Cost Comparison for Small Projects

For minimal configurations, Neon offers a free tier for databases up to 0.5 GB, while both AWS Aurora and traditional RDS would cost 2X-6X times more in a minimal configuration, without the scale-to-zero capability.

## Key Differences for Small Projects

Neon's architecture separates storage and compute, so resources auto-scale based on load. If your database sits idle, compute shuts down and costs drop to zero. This is particularly valuable for small projects with irregular usage patterns.

Serverless solutions like Neon are most cost-effective when usage is small and irregular, making it an ideal choice for small hobby and development projects.

# AWS Amplify Hosting Pricing

Based on the pricing page content, here are the specific costs:

## Build & Deploy Pricing
- **Standard Instance** (8GB/4vCPU): Free up to 1,000 minutes/month; "$0.01 per minute" thereafter

- **XLarge Instance** (72GB/36vCPU): "$0.10 per minute"

## Hosting Charges
- **Data Storage**: Free up to 5GB/month on CDN; "$0.023 per GB per month" for additional storage
- **Data Transfer Out**: Free up to 15GB/month; "$0.15 per GB served" beyond that

- **Request Count**: Free up to 500,000 requests/month; "$0.30 per 1 million requests" over limit

## Web Application Firewall
"$15/month per Amplify app" plus additional WAF costs

## Free Tier Note
New AWS customers receive up to $200 in free credits (as of July 15, 2025) applicable to Amplify services, available for 12 months of hosting deployment.

## AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) SSL Certificate Pricing

By default, ACM issues certificates that can only be used with services integrated with ACM are issued at no cost to you. However, if you issue an exportable public certificate, there is a charge at certificate issuance and again when the certificate renews.

For exportable public certificates, the pricing is:
- A certificate with one fully qualified domain name (FQDN) will cost $7 on issuance
- A certificate with one wildcard name (*.example.com) will cost $79

## AWS Route 53 Pricing

For Route 53 domain hosting and DNS services:

**Hosted Zones:** Hosted zone costs 25 cents per hosted zone per month

**Domain Registration:** A .com domain costs 12 bucks a year

**DNS Queries:** Queries on private hosted zones are provided at no additional cost to Route 53 customers, and Alias records are provided at no additional cost to Route 53 customers, if the Alias records are mapped to any of the following AWS services (including CloudFront, ELB, and others).

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of AWS Amplify vs Vercel for Next.js deployment in 2025-2026:

## Deployment Experience

Vercel offers the easiest deployment experience for Next.js developers with native SSR support, automatic scaling, and seamless integration. You can connect your GitHub repository, click Deploy, and have your app live on a .vercel.app domain in less than 5 minutes.

Amplify's automated process is incredibly simple, making it perfect for developers without extensive AWS experience, though there's a slight learning curve if you're new to the AWS ecosystem.

## Developer Experience Trade-offs

**Vercel's Advantages:**
- Vercel's "push-to-deploy" philosophy is integrated with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket, offering a seamless CI/CD experience
- Vercel is the team behind Next.js, so their platform is designed to handle it flawlessly with native SSR support and seamless integration

**Amplify's Advantages:**
- If your application needs video processing (MediaConvert), machine learning (SageMaker), or IoT integration, Amplify provides seamless access to the entire AWS ecosystem
- Amplify makes it seamless for front-end developers to deploy full-stack apps, especially with its built-in CI/CD pipeline

## Performance & Advanced Features

Vercel's integration with Next.js made it the fastest and easiest deployment platform with optimal performance. However, Amplify Generation 2 currently lacks Incremental Static Regeneration support, though this was promised for early 2025.

## Cost Considerations

AWS Amplify is 40% cheaper at scale, but requires more AWS expertise. Vercel's per-seat pricing ($20/month per developer) quickly adds up for teams with 3+ developers, while Amplify has zero per-seat costs. For most businesses with development teams, Amplify offers 30-75% cost savings, primarily by eliminating per-seat fees, though the trade-off is more configuration, slightly less polished developer experience, and lack of support for advanced Next.js features like Edge Functions.

## Use Case Recommendations

Choose AWS Amplify if you need integrated backend services (authentication, database, APIs), are building multi-platform applications, have compliance requirements (HIPAA, SOC 2), or have AWS expertise. Choose Vercel if you're using Next.js and want native optimizations, your frontend performance directly impacts revenue, prefer simple workflows, or have a small team without deep infrastructure expertise.

Based on the search results, I can provide you with comprehensive information about AWS Lambda's free tier:

## AWS Lambda Free Tier Overview

The AWS Lambda free tier includes one million free requests per month and 400,000 GB-seconds of compute time per month, usable for functions powered by both x86, and Graviton2 processors, in aggregate.

## Always Free Status

An important distinction: This free tier does not automatically expire at the end of your 12 month AWS Free Tier term, but is available indefinitely. This is different from some other AWS services that only offer 12 months of free tier benefits. Lambda's permanent free tier never expires, unlike EC2's 12-month limitation. Every AWS account receives 1 million requests monthly and 400,000 GB-seconds monthly, renewable each billing cycle.

## Additional Free Tier Benefits

Beyond requests and compute time, the free tier includes 100GiB of HTTP response streaming per month, beyond the first 6MB per request, which are free.

## Cost After Free Tier Limits

If you exceed these limits, you are charged $0.20 per 1 million requests thereafter and $0.00001667 for every GB-second used.

This free tier structure allows many small-scale applications and development workloads to run completely free of charge indefinitely.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about OpenNext for self-hosting Next.js on AWS Lambda with S3 and CloudFront in 2025-2026:

## Overview

OpenNext packages Next.js applications to run natively on AWS's serverless stack, mapping framework features onto services like Lambda, CloudFront, and S3, and translates routing, API handlers, and middleware into deployable artifacts, handling edge cases such as image optimization, ISR, and streaming responses.

## Key Architecture

SST relies on OpenNext as a bridge, an opensource adapter that effectively reverse-engineers the Next.js build output, translating Vercel-specific features into standard AWS primitives like Lambda functions and S3 buckets.

OpenNext automatically transforms the build output into infrastructure-ready assets—splitting static files for S3 and server-side logic into optimized Lambda functions.

## Important Considerations

The recommended approach is to embrace the S3 + CloudFront architecture for best performance and cost rather than trying to bundle everything into Lambda.

OpenNext is not a complete deployment solution—it's an adapter that requires proper infrastructure, and you can't just upload OpenNext output to Lambda and expect it to work.

## Recent Developments (2025-2026)

SST v3 (codenamed "Ion") has moved away from AWS CDK and CloudFormation, instead leveraging a high-performance infrastructure engine built on Pulumi and Terraform, which allows for sub-second updates and greater flexibility.

The search results indicate that as of January 2026, for Next.js on AWS Lambda in 2026, SST is recommended as the solution to work with OpenNext for production deployments.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about AWS CloudFront's always-free tier in 2025:

## AWS CloudFront Always-Free Tier (2025)

As of 2025, AWS CloudFront offers these free tier monthly limits: 1 TB (terabyte) data transfer out to the internet. This free tier is available indefinitely to all AWS users.

CloudFront's Free Tier offers 1 TB of data transfer out to the internet, and 10 million HTTP or HTTPS requests free each month.

Additional details about the free tier include:

- The free tier includes: 1 TB of data transfer out per month; 10 million HTTP/HTTPS requests; 2 million Lambda@Edge requests
- The pay-as-you-go pricing includes a 1TB perpetual free tier, meaning this benefit continues indefinitely after the initial 12-month free trial period ends

The free tier allows you to deliver substantial amounts of content globally without incurring charges as long as you stay within the 1TB and 10 million requests monthly limits.

Based on the search results, here's the information about AWS RDS free tier with db.t3.micro and db.t4g.micro instances for PostgreSQL:

## Free Tier Duration

If you signed up for AWS Free Tier prior to July 15, 2025, you can continue to use RDS for free for up to 12 months on select single-AZ instance databases with up to 750 hours per month. This is a **12-month free tier**, not an "always free" offering.

However, if you sign up for AWS Free Tier after July 15, 2025, you can choose between a Free Plan or a Paid Plan for access to RDS, with either plan offering $100 in credits and up to an additional $100 in credits for activating foundational AWS services.

## Instance Types and Hours

AWS provides 750 hours of Amazon RDS Single-AZ db.t3.micro and db.t4g.micro instances usage running MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL databases each month. If running more than one instance, usage is aggregated across instance classes.

## Additional Free Resources

You also receive 20 GB of General Purpose SSD storage (gp2) per month and 20 GB of automated database backup storage per month for 1 year.

In summary, it's a **12-month free tier** (not "always free") with 750 hours per month for eligible PostgreSQL instances.

Based on my search results, here's comprehensive information about Vercel pricing:

## Vercel Pro Pricing (2025-2026)

Additional paid seats on the Pro plan cost $20/month each, and the Pro plan starts with 1 included paid seat that can deploy projects, manage the team, and access all member-level permissions.

### Pro Plan Overview
In September 2025, the Pro plan was restructured to a flexible credit-based model: you get 1 TB Fast Data Transfer/month and 10 million Edge Requests/month, plus $20 in monthly credits applicable to any overage. The Pro plan includes all Hobby features in addition to $20 worth of monthly credits and higher infrastructure limits, plus collaboration features including shared environments, role-based access control, and free viewer seats for non-developers.

## Hobby Plan Limits

The Hobby plan is "the perfect starting place for your web app or personal project" and is free forever. It is strictly limited to personal and non-commercial use, and includes generous features (CDN, 1M edge requests/month, simple WAF) but very tight compute limits.

Specific Hobby limits include:
- Bandwidth is free but capped at 100 GB per month
- ISR reads are limited to 1M per month and ISR writes to 200K per month
- Functions can only run for up to 60 seconds (default), because the function duration is capped (and only slightly tunable) on free plans

## Key Pricing Differences

Vercel's tier structure follows a 10x scaling model between Hobby and Pro across most resources: bandwidth jumps from 100 GB to 1 TB, edge requests from 1 million to 10 million.

If you go beyond the 1 TB Pro plan limit, you need to pay $0.15 per extra GB.
